topic: An artist's work is displayed without their permission
raw_output:
[story 1]

The gallery lights felt like physical blows, searing the edges of my vision. I stood by the entrance, my knuckles white where I gripped the strap of my worn canvas bag. The canvases—my canvases—were hung on pristine white walls, bathed in a cool, indifferent glow. A murmur of polite, appreciative voices washed over me, a tide that seemed to pull the very air from my lungs. I watched a woman in silk, her smile practiced and effortless, point at a piece I had poured six months of my life into. A knot tightened in my chest, a cold, hard pressure that made breathing shallow. I wanted to scream, to shatter the glass separating me from that space, but the sound caught in my throat, reduced to a dry, rasping silence. My feet began to shuffle, a frantic, uneven rhythm against the polished floor, each step an acknowledgment of the profound wrongness of this tableau.

[story 2]

I found myself hovering near the back corner, pressed against a cold marble pillar. The air smelled of expensive perfume and stale champagne. Every glance that landed on my work felt like an accusation, sharp and undeniable. My hands were shaking so badly I had to press them against my thighs, trying to anchor myself to the reality that this was not mine. A deep, hollow ache settled beneath my ribs, a physical weight that made standing feel monumental. I watched patrons drift past, their conversations bright and carefree, completely oblivious to the raw, exposed nerve of my creation being admired by strangers. I took a slow, ragged breath, tasting dust and anxiety. I needed to move, to disappear, but my legs felt weighted with lead, refusing the simple command to flee.

[story 3]

"It’s… quite striking," a voice purred nearby. The compliment landed like a stone in my stomach, heavy and unforgiving. I forced a smile, a brittle thing that didn't reach my eyes, and retreated towards the shadowed alcove. The silence in my head was deafening, punctuated only by the faint, rhythmic click of a camera shutter capturing my likeness. I traced the outline of a nearby shadow with my gaze, trying to map an escape route through the crowded room. My jaw ached with tension, and I could feel a tremor running through my entire frame. Every muscle felt coiled, ready to spring, yet paralyzed by the sheer impossibility of reclaiming what was stolen. I just stood there, a statue carved from sudden, suffocating helplessness.
